core_behavior: The system must support the addition of compute nodes (servers, containers, or virtual machines) to increase capacity without requiring changes to application code, data schemas, or client interfaces.
Adding nodes must result in a measurable increase in throughput proportional to the resources added, up to defined limits.
The system must support increasing the CPU, memory, or storage resources of individual nodes without requiring downtime exceeding the defined maintenance window.
The system must automatically scale out (add capacity) when load exceeds defined thresholds and scale in (remove capacity) when load falls below defined thresholds, without human intervention.

Application logic components must be stateless. Any session or user state must be externalized to a shared, scalable state store (e.g., distributed cache or database) so that any instance can serve any request.

Incoming traffic must be distributed across all healthy instances using a load balancing mechanism. No single instance should receive a disproportionate share of traffic under normal operating conditions.
The data layer must support read scaling via read replicas or caching layers.
The data layer must support write scaling via sharding, partitioning, or distributed database mechanisms.
The data layer must support storage scaling without downtime, up to defined storage limits.
Long-running or resource-intensive operations must be offloaded to an asynchronous work queue, allowing the request-handling tier to remain responsive under high load.
inputs: Incoming requests: HTTP/S requests, API calls, message queue events, or equivalent
Scaling signals: CPU utilization, memory utilization, request queue depth, request latency, error rate, or custom metrics
Configuration parameters: Min/max instance counts, scaling thresholds, cooldown periods, resource limits
Infrastructure provisioning commands: Manual or automated commands to add/remove nodes
Traffic patterns: Scheduled or predicted load patterns used for proactive scaling
outputs: Processed responses: Correct responses to all requests regardless of which instance handles them
Scaling events: Logged and auditable records of every scale-out and scale-in action
Health metrics: Real-time metrics on instance count, resource utilization, latency, throughput, and error rate
Alerts: Notifications when scaling limits are reached, scaling fails, or thresholds are breached
Capacity reports: Periodic reports on resource utilization trends and scaling activity
assumptions: The system is deployed in a cloud or virtualized environment (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP, or private cloud with orchestration such as Kubernetes) that supports dynamic resource provisioning.
A 'normal' operating load is defined as up to 1,000 concurrent users or equivalent request rate. Scaling requirements are defined relative to this baseline.
The system must scale to at least 10× the baseline load (10,000 concurrent users) without architectural changes.
Individual services can be scaled independently rather than requiring the entire system to scale as a monolith.
Instances do not share in-process memory or local disk state for request handling.
A managed or dedicated load balancer (not application-level routing) is used at the ingress tier.
A monitoring and metrics collection system (e.g., Prometheus, CloudWatch, Datadog) is available and integrated.
failure_modes: Alerts are issued when scaling fails.
Alerts are issued when scaling limits are reached.
Alerts are issued when thresholds are breached.
performance_constraints: Adding nodes must result in a measurable increase in throughput proportional to the resources added, up to defined limits.
Vertical scaling must not require downtime exceeding the defined maintenance window.
The system must scale to at least 10× the baseline load (10,000 concurrent users) without architectural changes.
Storage scaling must occur without downtime, up to defined storage limits.
